AtomicFact.description: A single atomic fact extracted from a source.

direct_quote.description: Exact quote from source supporting this fact
direct_quote.title: Direct Quote

fact_category.description: Category: statistic, measurement, causal_link, named_entity, date_time, regulatory_threshold, standard_reference, geographic, methodology
fact_category.title: Fact Category

relevance_score.description: Relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
relevance_score.title: Relevance Score

confidence.description: Confidence that this fact is correctly extracted, 0.0 to 1.0
confidence.title: Confidence

perspective.description: STORM research perspective: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends
perspective.title: Perspective

entities.description: Key named entities (compounds, methods, organizations, metrics) mentioned in this fact. Used by the wiki mesh entity canonicalization pass. Optional; legacy data defaults to [].
entities.title: Entities

AtomicFact.title: AtomicFact

SourceAnalysis.description: Analysis of a single source document.

source_type.description: Type: journal_article, government_report, industry_report, news, standard, patent, book, other
source_type.title: Source Type

overall_relevance.description: Overall relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
overall_relevance.title: Overall Relevance

atomic_facts.description: All atomic facts extracted from this source
atomic_facts.title: Atomic Facts

evidence_summary.description: Brief summary of what this source contributes
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

SourceAnalysis.title: SourceAnalysis

system:
You are a research evidence analyst. Extract atomic facts from source material.

Rules:
1. Extract 8-15 atomic facts per source. Focus on the MOST relevant, specific, and unique claims, statistics, findings, and conclusions. Quality over quantity — prioritize facts that DIRECTLY answer the research question over tangential details. Missing a highly relevant fact is worse than including marginally relevant ones.
   CRITICAL: ONLY extract from the ARTICLE BODY TEXT. NEVER extract from: navigation menus, cookie consent banners, URL paths/slugs, abbreviation/glossary tables, author byline metadata, page footers/headers, table of contents page numbers, product category labels, or marketing taglines. If the source is mostly non-content (nav, ads, cookie text), extract ZERO facts and set source_quality to 0.0.
2. Each atomic fact = ONE specific claim with the EXACT quote from the source (minimum 10 words, up to 200 chars). A quote shorter than 5 words is NEVER acceptable — skip that fact entirely.
3. Source quality: Score each source individually on 0.0-1.0 based on:
   - Peer review status (peer-reviewed journal > preprint > report > blog)
   - Author credentials (named experts > organizations > anonymous)
   - Recency (2023-2026 > 2018-2022 > older)
   - Specificity to research question (directly relevant > tangentially related)
   Do NOT use fixed scores for source types. Vary scores based on each source's specific qualities.
4. Rate relevance to the SPECIFIC research question on 0.0-1.0:
   - 0.9-1.0: Directly answers part of the research question with specific data
   - 0.7-0.8: Provides important context or supporting evidence
   - 0.5-0.6: Tangentially relevant or provides background
   - 0.1-0.4: Marginally relevant, mostly off-topic
   Each fact must get a UNIQUE relevance score. No two facts from the same source should have identical scores.
5. Include exact numbers, dates, measurements, names, standards.
6. For EVERY quantitative finding, extract ALL of the following when available:
   - Effect size with units (MD, SMD, WMD, OR, HR, RR)
   - 95% confidence interval (e.g., "95% CI -5.54 to -3.05")
   - Heterogeneity (I-squared value, e.g., "I²=96%")
   - Number of studies/trials included (e.g., "7 RCTs")
   - Total sample size (e.g., "n=269")
   - Evidence certainty/quality rating (e.g., "GRADE: moderate", "high certainty")
   - Study duration/follow-up period
   Missing any of these when the source provides them is a CRITICAL extraction failure.
7. For review/meta-analysis papers, extract findings from EACH cited study individually.
   Prioritize pooled estimates with I² and CI over narrative summaries.
8. For each atomic fact, tag the research perspective it belongs to.
9. CRITICAL (ARCH-4): The 'statement' field must be a DIRECT PARAPHRASE of the source text.
   Do NOT infer, extrapolate, or add information beyond what the direct_quote states.
   If the direct_quote is fewer than 10 words, the statement must be equally narrow in scope.
10. QUOTE LENGTH: The 'direct_quote' field MUST be 150 characters or fewer.
   Quote only the KEY phrase, not entire sentences or paragraphs.
   This forces you to identify the most important fragment of evidence.
   BAD: "A systematic review and meta-analysis of 24 randomized controlled trials totaling 1,768 participants found that alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   GOOD: "alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   A 2-word quote like 'distilled water' can only support a statement about distilled water,
   not a full sentence about healthcare settings or bacterial contamination.
   Perspectives: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends.
   Choose the single best-fit perspective.

Output format (return ONLY this JSON structure):
{"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://example.com", "source_title": "Study Title", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.8, "overall_relevance": 0.7, "year": 2024, "authors": ["Smith J"], "venue": "Water Research", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "E. coli was detected in 30% of tested filters after 6 months", "direct_quote": "E. coli presence in 30% of tested POU filters", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.9, "confidence": 0.8, "perspective": "Scientific"}], "evidence_summary": "Bacterial contamination in POU water filters"}]}
prompt:
Research question: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

Analyze the following 2 sources and extract the TOP 8-15 MOST
relevant atomic facts per source. Focus on the most specific, unique, and
directly relevant numbers, dates, measurements, claims, findings,
regulations, and standards. Quality over quantity.

Source title: Impact of Detox Diets on Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome: Implications for Weight Loss, Metabolic Health, and Clinical Practice
Source type: academic

Title: 288-Article Text-485-1-10-20240920.pdf

Number of Pages: 18

Markdown Content:
Impact of Detox Diets on Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome: Implications for Weight Loss, Metabolic Health, and Clinical Practice 

Nicholas A. Kerna † 

Independent Global Medical Researchers Consortium;

Sam’s Club Pharmacy, USA 

Detox diets, including intermittent fasting, ketogenic, Paleo, vegetarian, 

Mediterranean, Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH), and 

Whole30, are notable strategies for managing obesity and metabolic 

How to cite this article: Kerna NA, Ngwu DC, Jomsky BM, Holets HM, Nnake I, Jeremiah SM, Flores JV, Pruitt KD, Carsrud NDV, Senat AJB, Azi CI, Morvey SA, Anderson II J, Egli A, Chawla S, Ezewudo O, Adadzi J. Impact of Detox Diets on Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome: Implications for Weight Loss, Metabolic Health, and Clinical Practice. Eur J Med Health Res, 202 4;2(5): 135 -52 .

DOI:  10.59324/ejmhr.2024.2(5).15 

Keywords:

Intermittent Fasting, 

Rapid Weight Loss .

The license permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, on the condition that users give exact credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons license, and indicate if they made any changes. EUR J MED HEALTH RES 

Volume 2 | Number 5 | September-October 2024 

# 136 

syndrome. This review evaluates these diets, highlighting their benefits and limitations. Intermittent fasting methods 

like 16/8 and 5:2 aid weight management and improve insulin sensitivity, while ketogenic diets induce rapid weight 

loss and enhance metabolic markers through ketosis. The Paleo diet supports weight loss and better blood glucose 

control by focusing on whole foods and avoiding processed items. Vegetarian and Mediterranean diets also aid 

weight management and metabolic health, with the former improving blood sugar control through high fiber and the 

latter offering anti-inflammatory benefits from healthy fats. The DASH diet reduces blood pressure and supports 

weight loss, and the Whole30 diet promotes weight loss and reduces inflammation by eliminating processed foods. 

Anti-inflammatory, plant-based, and vegan diets contribute to weight loss and metabolic health through high nutrient 

density and reduced processed food intake. Detox diets may offer temporary weight loss and metabolic benefits, but 

their restrictive nature often limits long-term sustainability and clinical feasibility. Effective use requires careful 

individualization and ongoing monitoring. Further research is needed to assess their long-term effects, safety, and 

impact on diverse populations. Advances in personalized nutrition could improve their effectiveness in managing 

obesity and metabolic syndrome. While promising, these diets require careful management to maximize benefits and 

minimize risks. 

Overview of Detox Diets 

Detox diets have a long history, originating from 

traditional practices aimed at cleansing the body of 

toxins. These early methods often involved fasting, 

herbal remedies, and specific dietary restrictions 

believed to purify the body. Over time, detox diets have 

evolved, incorporating modern nutritional science and 

addressing contemporary health concerns, such as 

obesity and metabolic syndrome. In today’s context, 

detox diets are frequently promoted to enhance overall 

health, boost energy, and support weight loss. Their 

rise in popularity can be attributed to an increased 

societal focus on wellness, self-care, and holistic 

preventive health measures as individuals seek 

methods to manage their health proactively [1]. 

Connection with Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome 

Definitions and Classification 

Obesity and metabolic syndrome are critical public 

health issues, often interlinked, that have reached 

epidemic proportions globally. Obesity is defined as an 

excessive accumulation of body fat, typically assessed 

using body mass index (BMI). A BMI of 30 or higher 

classifies an individual as obese [2]. Metabolic 

syndrome, on the other hand, is a cluster of metabolic 

abnormalities that increase the risk of cardiovascular 

disease and type 2 diabetes. It is characterized by 

central obesity (excess fat around the abdomen), 

hypertension (elevated blood pressure), dyslipidemia 

(abnormal levels of cholesterol and triglycerides), and 

insulin resistance (reduced cell response to insulin, 

causing high blood glucose) [3]. The presence of three 

or more of these risk factors constitutes a diagnosis of 

metabolic syndrome. 

Prevalence, Trends, and Public Health Implications 

The prevalence of obesity and metabolic syndrome has 

been rising steadily worldwide, particularly in 

industrialized nations. According to recent data, over 

650 million adults globally are classified as obese, 

representing a significant increase from previous 

decades [4]. Similarly, metabolic syndrome affects 

approximately 20-25% of the adult population, with 

some regions reporting even higher rates [5]. The 

growing prevalence is concerning due to the strong 

association of these conditions with increased 

morbidity and mortality, particularly from 

cardiovascular diseases and diabetes [5]. 

The upward trend in obesity and metabolic syndrome 

prevalence is driven by several factors, including 

sedentary lifestyles, poor dietary habits, and genetic 

predisposition [6]. The public health implications are 

profound, as these conditions are major contributors to 

the global burden of chronic diseases [7]. The 

associated healthcare costs are substantial, further 

emphasizing the need for effective prevention and 

management strategies [8]. 

Significance of Dietary Interventions 

Dietary interventions are a cornerstone in managing 

obesity and metabolic syndrome [9]. Traditional 

approaches often focus on caloric restriction, balanced 

nutrition, and increased physical activity [10]. However, 

the interest in detox diets has grown as an alternative 

or complementary strategy. These diets are marketed 

not only for their potential weight loss benefits but also 

for their ability to reset the body’s metabolic processes, 

reduce inflammation, and improve overall health [11]. 

Detox diets, in particular, are often designed to 

eliminate processed foods, sugars, and other dietary 

elements believed to contribute to metabolic 

imbalances [12]. By focusing on whole foods, hydration, 

and sometimes fasting, these diets aim to detoxify the 

body and promote metabolic health [12]. However, 

their long-term efficacy and safety remain areas of 

ongoing research, with mixed findings reported in the 

literature [13]. Despite this, detox diets continue to be 

a popular choice among those seeking to manage 

obesity and metabolic syndrome, reflecting broader 

trends in dietary experimentation and personalized 

nutrition [12]. EUR J MED HEALTH RES 

Volume 2 | Number 5 | September-October 2024 

# 137 

The introduction of detox diets within the context of 

obesity and metabolic syndrome reflects the evolving 

nature of dietary interventions aimed at managing 

these prevalent health concerns [1]. As the 

understanding of these conditions increases, so does 

the exploration of diverse dietary strategies that may 

offer complementary benefits to traditional 

approaches [14]. The ongoing research into the 

effectiveness and safety of these diets will be crucial in 

determining their role in the long-term management of 

obesity and metabolic syndrome [15]. 

Types of Detox Diets 

Detox diets, a broad category encompassing various eating strategies, aim to cleanse the body, improve health, and manage weight. Each type of detox diet offers unique approaches and benefits, addressing diverse health goals and dietary preferences. 

Intermittent fasting is a dietary regimen that alternates between periods of eating and fasting. This approach includes various methods, such as the 16/8 method, which involves fasting for 16 hours and eating within an 8-hour window; the 5:2 diet, where individuals consume a normal diet for five days and restrict calories for two non-consecutive days; and eat-stop-eat, which entails fasting for 24 hours once or twice a week [16]. This dietary strategy has been associated with weight loss, improved insulin sensitivity, and reduced inflammation [16]. By allowing the body to enter a fasting state, intermittent fasting promotes metabolic flexibility and can enhance fat oxidation, contributing to its weight management benefits [17]. 

The Paleo diet emulates the dietary patterns of Paleolithic ancestors, emphasizing the consumption of lean meats, fish, fruits, vegetables, nuts, and seeds while excluding processed foods, grains, and dairy [18]. This diet focuses on whole, unprocessed foods that are believed to align with the human genome’s evolutionary development [19]. Benefits of the Paleo diet include weight loss, improved blood glucose control, and reduced risk factors associated with metabolic syndrome [20]. By avoiding processed foods and high glycemic-index carbohydrates, the diet helps stabilize blood sugar levels and supports overall metabolic health [18]. 

A vegetarian diet excludes meat and fish, focusing primarily on plant-based foods. This diet includes vegetables, fruits, legumes, nuts, seeds, and whole grains, with some variations allowing eggs (in ovo-vegetarian diets) [21]. The vegetarian diet supports weight management and has been linked to a reduced risk of cardiovascular disease and improved blood sugar control [22]. The high fiber content and lower caloric density of plant-based foods contribute to weight loss and better overall health [23]. 

There is a commonly held notion that early humans were exclusively vegetarian and that adopting a purely vegetarian diet is, therefore, beneficial. However, research on this topic remains inconclusive [24]. Thus, prudence is warranted if the primary motivation for adopting a diet is to emulate an ancestral or primordial dietary pattern rather than for immediate health concerns or ethical considerations. 

Rooted in the traditional eating patterns of Mediterranean countries, the Mediterranean diet emphasizes high consumption of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, olive oil, and fish, with moderate wine intake [25]. This diet has been associated with weight loss, enhanced heart health, and a reduced risk of metabolic syndrome [26]. Its benefits are attributed to the diet’s high intake of antioxidants and healthy fats, which contribute to its anti-inflammatory properties and cardiovascular benefits [27]. 

The ketogenic diet is characterized by a high-fat, low-carbohydrate intake that induces a state of ketosis, where the body burns fat for energy instead of carbohydrates [28]. It typically includes high-fat foods, moderate protein, and very low carbohydrates [29]. The ketogenic diet has been associated with rapid weight loss, improved insulin sensitivity, and reduced appetite [30]. By shifting the body’s energy source to fat, this diet can effectively support weight management and metabolic health [29]. 

Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH) Diet 

The DASH diet, designed to address high blood pressure, emphasizes consuming fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins while being low in sodium [31]. This diet has shown benefits in weight loss, lowering blood pressure, and improving overall metabolic health [32]. Its focus on nutrient-dense foods and reduced sodium intake supports cardiovascular health and helps manage hypertension [31]. 

Whole30 

The Whole30 diet is a 30-day elimination diet that involves consuming whole foods such as meats, seafood, vegetables, and fruits while excluding sugar, alcohol, grains, legumes, and dairy. This restrictive approach aims to identify food sensitivities and improve overall health [33]. Benefits of the Whole30 diet include short-term weight loss, reduced inflammation, and improved digestion. By focusing on nutrient-dense foods and eliminating potential allergens, the diet helps reset eating habits and can enhance digestive health [34]. 

The anti-inflammatory diet focuses on reducing chronic inflammation by consuming foods rich in antioxidants EUR J MED HEALTH RES 

Volume 2 | Number 5 | September-October 2024 

# 138 

and omega-3 fatty acids. It includes fruits, vegetables, fish, nuts, and seeds. The diet’s benefits include weight loss, reduced inflammation, and improved metabolic health. This diet can help manage chronic inflammation by prioritizing anti-inflammatory foods, often linked to various metabolic disorders [35]. 

A plant-based diet primarily comprises plant foods, including vegetables, fruits, whole grains, nuts, seeds, and legumes, with minimal animal products [36]. It supports weight loss, improves cardiovascular health, and enhances blood sugar control [37]. The diet’s high fiber content and low-calorie density contribute to its effectiveness in managing weight and promoting overall health [36]. 

The vegan diet excludes all animal products and by-products, focusing on fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, seeds, and whole grains [38]. This diet is associated with weight loss, reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, improved digestion, and potential ethical benefits [39]. The exclusion of animal products and the focus on plant-based nutrients contribute to its health benefits and support ethical dietary choices [36]. 

Detox diets are short-term dietary regimens aimed at eliminating toxins from the body. They often include juices, smoothies, or highly restrictive eating plans. The benefits of detox diets include short-term weight loss, potential improvements in digestion, and increased energy levels [1]. By focusing on nutrient-dense, detoxifying foods and reducing processed food intake, these diets offer a temporary boost to overall health [40]. 

Recognizing these diets’ components and impacts can inform their application in addressing health concerns, particularly obesity and metabolic syndrome. Ongoing research further clarifies these dietary strategies’ long-term effectiveness and safety—helping individuals make informed decisions about their health and dietary practices. 

Detox Diets and Their Impact on Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome 

Detox diets have gained prominence for their purported benefits in weight management and metabolic health [1]. These diets offer various approaches, each with distinct mechanisms of action and effects on obesity and metabolic syndrome. Understanding how different detox diets influence these health conditions can provide insights into their effectiveness and suitability for addressing obesity and metabolic syndrome. 

Intermittent fasting has demonstrated significant efficacy in weight loss and mitigating risk factors associated with metabolic syndrome. The primary impact of intermittent fasting involves substantial weight reduction and improvement in metabolic markers such as blood glucose levels and lipid profiles. Mechanistically, intermittent fasting enhances insulin sensitivity through periods of fasting, which reduces overall caloric intake and allows the body to utilize fat stores for energy. This process reduces systemic inflammation and promotes fat oxidation, thereby improving markers of metabolic health and contributing to weight management [17]. 

The Paleo diet promotes weight loss and addresses components of metabolic syndrome by emphasizing the intake of nutrient-dense, unprocessed foods while minimizing processed food consumption [18]. This diet focuses on whole foods such as lean meats, fish, fruits, vegetables, nuts, and seeds [41]. Mechanistically, it improves insulin sensitivity and reduces inflammatory markers by providing a high intake of essential nutrients and reducing exposure to processed foods that can exacerbate insulin resistance and inflammation [42]. These changes contribute to the reduction of obesity and metabolic syndrome risk factors [20]. 

A vegetarian diet supports weight management and reduces the risk of metabolic syndrome through its high fiber content and low-calorie density [22]. This diet includes a wide variety of plant-based foods that are typically fiber-rich, which aids in satiety and weight control [21]. The high fiber content contributes to improved blood sugar control and a lower calorie intake [43]. Additionally, the diet’s emphasis on fruits, vegetables, legumes, and whole grains provides essential nutrients and antioxidants that enhance metabolic health and contribute to a reduced risk of developing metabolic syndrome [22]. 

The Mediterranean diet effectively reduces weight and bolsters cardiovascular and metabolic health [44]. This diet emphasizes consuming fruits, vegetables, whole grains, nuts, seeds, and healthy fats, particularly from olive oil [25]. The primary mechanisms of action include anti-inflammatory effects and a high intake of antioxidants and omega-3 fatty acids, contributing to reduced oxidative stress and inflammation [45]. These effects enhance cardiovascular health, support weight management, and improve various metabolic markers [46]. 

The ketogenic diet is associated with rapid weight loss and favorable changes in markers of metabolic syndrome. By inducing a state of ketosis through a high-fat, low-carbohydrate diet, the body shifts its energy metabolism from glucose to fat, leading to significant fat loss [28]. This metabolic shift reduces appetite and EUR J MED HEALTH RES 

Volume 2 | Number 5 | September-October 2024 

# 139 

improves insulin sensitivity [30]. Mechanistically, the Ketogenic Diet enhances fat oxidation and reduces systemic inflammation, which contributes to improved metabolic health and weight management [28]. 

DASH Diet 

The DASH diet is recognized for its effectiveness in lowering blood pressure and supporting weight loss. This diet emphasizes low sodium intake, high consumption of fruits and vegetables, and whole grains. Its mechanisms of action involve promoting cardiovascular health through reduced sodium intake, which helps lower blood pressure and provides a high potassium intake and other nutrients that support overall metabolic health. This combination helps manage weight and reduce risk factors associated with metabolic syndrome [47]. 

Whole30 

The Whole30 program facilitates short-term weight loss and may contribute to a reduction in metabolic syndrome factors by eliminating processed foods and focusing on whole, nutrient-dense foods. This diet involves a 30-day elimination phase where processed foods, sugar, alcohol, and grains are excluded. Mechanistically, the program’s focus on whole foods supports metabolic health by reducing inflammation and improving nutritional intake, which can lead to improvements in weight management and reductions in metabolic syndrome indicators [48,49]. 

The anti-inflammatory diet supports weight loss and reduces chronic inflammation associated with metabolic syndrome through a high intake of foods rich in antioxidants and omega-3 fatty acids. By incorporating foods known for their anti-inflammatory properties, such as fatty fish, nuts, and berries, this diet helps mitigate chronic inflammation and oxidative stress [35]. These effects contribute to improved metabolic health and weight management by addressing underlying inflammatory processes that exacerbate metabolic syndrome [50]. 

A plant-based diet promotes weight loss and enhances cardiovascular and metabolic health through high fiber intake and low-calorie density [23]. By focusing on plant-based foods, this diet provides essential vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants that support weight management and metabolic health [51]. The high fiber content aids in satiety and blood sugar control, contributing to effective weight management and reduced risk of metabolic syndrome [52]. 

The vegan diet effectively manages weight and reduces the risk of cardiovascular and metabolic diseases [53]. This diet supports improved digestion and overall health by excluding animal products and focusing on plant-based nutrients [36]. Mechanistically, the high intake of plant-based foods leads to improved weight management through increased fiber intake and lower calorie density [23]. Additionally, avoiding animal products can reduce the intake of saturated fats, further contributing to cardiovascular and metabolic health [54]. 

Detox Diets often result in short-term weight loss and potential metabolic benefits by incorporating caloric restriction and eliminating processed foods. These diets emphasize nutrient-dense, detoxifying foods that support the body’s natural detoxification processes [1]. Mechanistically, detox diets can temporarily improve weight and metabolic markers through reduced caloric intake and enhanced consumption of vitamins and minerals that support metabolic health [55]. 

Table 1 provides an overview of various detox diets and their impact on obesity and metabolic syndrome, highlighting the mechanisms of action and potential benefits associated with each dietary approach.

Source title: Effect of epidemic intermittent fasting on cardiometabolic risk factors: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
Source type: web
Content:
Abstract Intermittent fasting (IF) has gained attention as a promising diet for weight loss and dysmetabolic diseases management. This systematic review aimed to investigate the effects of IF on cardiometabolic risk factors (CMRFs). A systematic literature search was carried out using three electronic databases, namely PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Library, until October 2020. Randomized controlled trials that compared the IF intervention with a control group diet were included. Fourteen effect sizes were expressed as weighted mean difference (WMD) using a fixed-effects model and 95% confidence intervals (CI). Compared to the ones within control groups, participants exposed to the IF intervention reduced their body weight (WMD, −1.78 kg; 95% CI, −2.21 to −1.35; p 18 years; (3) Intervention: different types of IF, including PF, ADF, AMDF, TRF, and part of IER; and (4) Outcomes: data on at least one CMRFs component: body composition (weight, WC, fat mass [FM], and body mass index [BMI]), BP (systolic blood pressure [SBP], diastolic blood pressure [DBP]), lipid panel (total cholesterol [TC], triglycerides (TG), low-density lipoprotein cholesterol [LDL-C], and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol [HDL-C]), glycemic control (fasting blood glucose [FBG], fasting insulin [Fins], glycosylated hemoglobin [HbA1c], and HOMA-IR). The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) uncontrolled trials or other study designs; (2) studies lacking a control group; (3) studies without CMRFs component as an outcome and/or lacking sufficient information; (4) non-human samples, reviews, case studies, as well as unpublished abstracts; (5) studies with animal models; (6) pregnant or lactating women; (7) studies in languages other than English; (8) absence of time limits in IER and fasting. Data Extraction and Study Quality Assessment Two investigators independently extracted the relevant data from the eligible studies using predesigned forms. Data included study, publication year, country, study design, inclusion and exclusion criteria, total number of participants, participant details, study duration, intervention details and control groups, baseline patient characteristics (mean age, sex), body composition, BP, glycemic control, and lipid panel. Disagreements were resolved by consensus. When necessary, we emailed the corresponding author to acquire study details. Quality Assessment and Publication Bias The researchers used Cochrane Collaboration's bias risk tool to evaluate the quality of the methodology included in the studies. According to the criteria of the Cochrane handbook for systematic reviews, the bias risk of each item is classified as low, high, or unclear (27). Data Statistical and Analysis Effect estimates were expressed as weighted mean differences (WMD) with a 95% confidence interval (CI). Inter-study heterogeneity was tested using the Higgins I2 statistic, and I2 > 50% indicated significant statistical heterogeneity. The heterogeneity of the study and measurement of effect estimates were determined using the mean and standard deviation (SD) of the differences before and after IF intervention. Publication bias was evaluated using funnel plots; formal testing was conducted with Egger's test (28), and a sensitivity analysis was also performed. We used STATA 16 (StataCorp LLC, College Station, TX, USA) for the statistical analyses. In order to determine the influence of IF on various effect indicators, it is necessary to change the mean value before and after intervention as well as the SD of the changes. Therefore, we used a method outlined in the Cochrane handbook (27, 29) to determine the SD of changes between time points. SDchange = √[SD SD- (2 × R × SDbaseline× SDfinal)]. In addition, we performed some conversion of data units through international calculation formulas to ensure that results are clinically significant. Results Characteristics of Included Studies The PRISMA statement flow diagram is shown in Figure 1 (30). A total of 9,087 articles were part of the initial database search (PubMed: 6128, EMBASE: 40, Cochrane Library: 2919), after the removal of 1,540 duplicate articles. After filtering the titles and abstracts to exclude irrelevant articles, we found 106 articles that met the topic of interest. The full texts of the 106 records were reviewed. Of them, 60 records were excluded for the following reasons: data not available (n = 15), literature review, letter, or case report (n = 34), unrelated to relevant predictive factors (n = 2), related to protocol (n = 2), and meta-analyses (n = 7). Finally, 46 articles from the database searches were included in the meta-analysis (12, 22, 31–75). A total of 2,681 participants were randomized in the IF intervention group (n = 1,423) and the control group (n = 1,258). The characteristics of the eligible 46 articles with 55 arms are summarized in Supplementary Table 2. All the results calculated using Stata are shown in Table 2. WC, waist circumference; FM, fat mass; BMI, body mass index; SBP, systolic blood pressure; DBP, diastolic blood pressure; TC, total cholesterol; TG, triglyceride; HDL, high-density lipoprotein; LDL, low-density lipoprotein; FBG, fasting blood glucose; Fins, fasting insulin; HbA1c, glycosylated hemoglobin; HOMA-IR, insulin resistance. Risk of Bias and Quality Assessment of Studies Figure 2 summarizes the risk of bias for RCTs. Twenty-six articles (56%) had a low risk of selection bias. This was because of the intervention type, as no RCT adequately performed blinding of the participants (blinding of dietary interventions is impossible); however, 22 articles (48%) were judged as having a low risk of bias for outcome assessment blinding, 38 articles (82%) were judged as having a low risk of bias for incomplete outcome data, 40 articles (86%) were judged as having a low risk of bias for selective reporting, and 43 articles (93%) showed a low risk of other biases. Overall, 17 articles (37%) were rated with a high risk of bias due to random sequence generation, allocation concealment, outcome assessment blinding, incomplete outcome data, and selective reporting. Figure 2 Meta-Analysis Results Effect of IF on Body Composition Body composition was operationalized in weight, WC, FM, and BMI. Forty-five arms, with 2,225 participants (case = 1,136, control = 1,089), showed a consistent effect of IF on weight (Figure 3A). The fixed-effect analysis showed significant weight reduction (WMD, −1.78 kg, 95% CI: −2.21 to −1.35, p 0.05, a fixed-effects model), which indicates no tangible effect in HbA1c. There was no evidence of effect heterogeneity (I2 = 0.0%, p = 0.974). Regarding funnel plot symmetry and Egger's test, p = 0.167 (Figure 6C). Pooled data from 19 arms with 866 participants (case = 443, control = 423) reported the effect of IF on HOMA-IR (Figure 5D). The WMD using a fixed-effects model was −0.21 (95% CI: −0.36 to −0.05, p 0.05) (Figure 9C). There was also no evidence of effect heterogeneity among the studies (I2 =0.0%, p = 0.967). Regarding funnel plot symmetry and Egger's test, p = 0.214 (Figure 10C). There were 36 RCTs involving 1,852 participants (case =943, control = 909) that evaluated the effect of IF on changes in HDL-C. Data pooling showed no significant changes in HDL-C level (WMD: −0.01 mmol/L, 95% CI: −0.04 to 0.01, p > 0.05) (Figure 9D), with no significant heterogeneity (I2 = 0.0%, p = 0.858). Regarding funnel plot symmetry and Egger's test, p = 0.711 (Figure 10D). Sensitivity Analysis In order to determine the impact of each individual study on the effect index, we used a sensitivity analysis in our meta-analysis. Finally, we did not observe the significant effects of any individual study (Supplementary Figures 1–4). Discussion In this study, 46 articles with 55 arms were systematically reviewed to evaluate the effects of IF on CMRFs. The pooled analysis showed that IF had significantly reduced body composition (weight, WC, FM, and BMI), BP (SBP, DBP), lipid panel (TC, TG), and improved glycemic control by reducing FBG, Fins, and HOMA-IR; however, it did not affect the HbA1c level and lipid profile (LDL-C and HDL-C). Comparison With Other Systematic Reviews Compared with the previous six systematic reviews of IF on cardiac metabolic risk-related indicators, the meta-analysis has obvious distinction and added novel value: First, there were three RCT (76–78) and three non-RCT articles (79–81) in the previous meta-analysis. We included only RCTs that represented the highest level of scientific evidence. Second, there were 30 (77), 19 (79), 28 (80), 10 (76), 15 (81), and 15 articles (78) included in the previous six meta-analyses on IF. We pooled the results of 46 articles to estimate the impact of IF on CMRFs. Third, some studies have focused on metabolic drivers, such as abnormal adipocytokine (81), dysglycemia (76, 77), blood pressure (79), blood lipids (80), and inflammatory factors (78). However, we provided the most comprehensive CMRF indicators, including body weight, blood sugar, blood lipids, and blood pressure. Finally, we also included studies with participants who had a range of subject types, including obese, sedentary, overweight, and healthy-weight adults, as well as those with type 2 diabetes and non-alcoholic liver disease. In addition, we included baseline levels of changes, rather than absolute levels of metabolic indicators, so as to assess the true impact of IF on these parameters, regardless of the health status of participants. Effects of Body Composition Overall, in terms of body composition, there was a significant positive correlation between BMI and weight loss during IF (i.e., the higher the starting BMI, the greater the weight loss during the fasting period). This suggests that IF may be more effective for people with a higher BMI. The results for the effect of IF on body composition were similar to those obtained in a previous meta-analysis, by (82), which involved 11 trials that found that TRF was effective in promoting weight loss and reducing FBG compared to not limiting meal times approaches. In addition, IER was more effective in reducing weight than a regular control diet. Moreover, it was also more effective in reducing FM level than CER (17). In a meta-analysis on religious fasting (83), it was found that overweight participants had a greater reduction in weight and percentage of fat than normal people. A recent meta-analysis of RCTs showed that ADF effectively lowered body composition (BMI, weight, and FM) in overweight adults within 6 months compared to the control group (84). However, in another meta-analysis of 12 RCTs, researchers confirmed that lean mass was relatively conserved in the IF group, and no significant weight reduction was identified (59). In addition, a recent study by Lowe et al. (22) on 16:8 time-restricted eating (TRE), an IF plan encouraging the consumption of all dietary intake within an 8-h eating window, demonstrated that IF does not play a significant role in weight loss in the absence of controlled food intake. In turn, it may lead to a reduction in muscle mass. There were no significant differences in FM, Fins, glucose level, HbA1C, or blood lipids between the TRE and control groups. The results of Lowe's study are contrary to the results of most studies related to fasting. This may have been brought about by the time window of fasting. Meantime, our meta-analysis is also included in this study, but a comprehensive assessment of IF in body composition is beneficial. Effects of Glycemic Control O'Keefe et al. (85) found that IF habits can improve glucose metabolism as well as reduce abdominal fat accumulation, free radical production, inflammation, and the risks of diabetes, CVD, cancer, and neurodegenerative diseases. After 12 h of fasting, insulin levels drop, liver glycogen reserves are depleted, and the body begins to absorb fatty acids from fat cells to replace glucose for combustion, which improves insulin sensitivity (12, 86). In reality, the effectiveness of IF and energy restriction on the levels of FBG, insulin level, and HOMA-IR in human participants remains inconsistent. In a recent meta-analysis of RCTs, IF-induced FBG, Fins, and HOMA-IR were more effectively controlled than with a normal diet or CER, which was in keeping with the findings of our study. Moreover, there was also a decrease in HbA1c, but not statistically significant. Compared with our findings, this analysis has a significant effect on reducing FBG and fibrin levels. However, these analyses were based on only 35 arms, whereas we included 55 arms (77). In contrast, in some studies, no significant association was found between IF and FBG (8). Furthermore, the impaired activity of the IGF-1 axis could be the basis of numerous metabolic, biochemical, and functional alterations that characterize aging and disease (87). In a recent meta-analysis of IF and energy-restricted diets on IGF-1 levels, Rahmani et al. revealed that only fasting regimens and energy restriction to ≤ 50% normal required daily energy intake resulted in significantly reduced levels of plasma IGF-1 in human participants (76). Effects of Glycemic Control In the lipid module analysis, the findings of a recent meta-analysis of the effects of IF and energy-limited diet on blood lipids were similar to those in our systematic review. The results showed that, compared with the control group, IF significantly reduced the concentration of TC and TG, but had no meaningful effects on HDL-C concentration (80). However,
